kept.text:
$ASTS $LMT: Pictured below is a @LockheedMartin TPY-4 radar which uses phased array technology. In this case, the radar uses L-band in the 1,215–1,400 MHz frequency range. As explained by Lockheed in the previous link, sub 2 GHz is considered "low band" for radar technology. (It's a bit different for cellular which considers sub 1 GHz as "low band")

However, as you can see in the photo, this radar is ground-based. It is fixed in one location and meant to provide early warning only at specific locations, such as strategic coasts. Most importantly it is GIGANTIC. A GIGANTIC PHASED ARRAY situated on the ground.

What @AST_SpaceMobile is doing is placing GIGANTIC low band radars into Low Earth Orbit to provide an EARLY WARNING SYSTEM that covers the ENTIRE GLOBE. They call these space-based radars BlueBird satellites.

Yes, the same satellites that will extend everyday cellular coverage. They are DUAL-USE for commercial and government.

2/6

kept.text:
Oil has dozens of global suppliers. When one source gets disrupted, others fill the gap.

Domestic rare earth alloys are different. There is no backup supplier. There is no strategic reserve. If rare earth alloys disappeared tomorrow, the F-35 production line at Lockheed Martin would go silent. EVs stop rolling off the line. Missile guidance systems, radar arrays, wind turbines - all of it depends on permanent magnets made from rare earth metals that almost no one outside China can produce.

We still cannot imagine the scale of the problem and the concentration of rare earth manufacturing in China. It isn't 30% or 50%; it goes above 90%.

Neodymium surged to the $130-150/kg range in China, while quoted metal prices outside China were already around $220/kg.

Dysprosium prices in China hit the ~$240–250/kg range in March.

Terbium is now holding in the $1,000-1,100/kg range, and at a multiple of every other rare earth used in high-performance magnets.

Outside of China, everyone’s paying a massive premium for rare earths. Europe and the U.S. are sometimes paying 2.5-3X of these prices. Neodymium moves closer to the $210-$200 range, while Terbium and Dysprosium shoot through the roof, towards $900/kg for Dysprosium and over $4,000/kg for Terbium, depending on its form.

@realloys NASDAQ: $ALOY is the only rare earths manufacturer that is not building into China prices, but into the premium outside China.

REalloys operates a facility in Euclid, Ohio, with 30 years of metallurgy expertise, active Pentagon contracts, and a proven capability in the Western Hemisphere to metallize rare earths

Their operations are scaling, especially around their partnership with the Saskatchewan Research Council (SRC), which is building North America's first fully integrated, commercial rare earth processing facility.

And they are still the only ones ready for January 1, 2027, when new U.S. defense procurement rules under DFARS and 10 U.S.C. §4872 take effect. After that date, Chinese-origin rare earth materials cannot be used in American defense systems.

Pay attention.

Know more: https://t.co/BSCqgkQpPf

quoted_tweet.text:
$NOK just got embedded inside Anduril’s Sentry stack and the market has barely reacted.

Think about what that actually means.

Anduril is the most important defense technology company being built right now. Their autonomous systems are being deployed across every branch of the US military. 

The Sentry tower is their battlefield surveillance and communications platform. It deploys in under 3 hours with zero infrastructure required.

Nokia’s radio technology is now the communications backbone inside it.

That is not a telecom equipment sale. That is a defense contract with the company that is redefining how wars are fought. Every Sentry tower that deploys anywhere in the world runs Nokia inside it.

And this is just the Anduril piece.

Nokia is simultaneously inside Nvidia’s AI-RAN stack, supplying hyperscaler optical backbone infrastructure through Infinera, and now embedded in autonomous defense systems. 

Three completely different industries discovering the same company at the same time.

Trading at 12x forward earnings.

$NOK

tweet.text:
Lockheed Martin Forced To “Get Creative” As Rare Earth Shortages Hit Defense Supply Chains $LMT $MP $NB $USAR 

Executives from Lockheed Martin, Oerlikon Surface Solutions and e-Vac Magnetics discussed how they mitigate costs and addressed their concerns in procuring critical elements in the defense industrial base at the Safe Summit conference on Tuesday.

Samuel Stein, a critical materials engineer at Lockheed Martin, said the defense contractor doesn’t buy raw materials but purchases components made with raw materials incorporated into its systems.

However, the company had to “get creative” to mitigate the timing as it takes a while for them to receive the component. 
Thus, the company works with its sub-tier suppliers to coordinate timing and delivery.
“When a rare earth comes out of the ground, it doesn’t get onto an F-35 for two to three years,” Stein said. 

https://t.co/wav8s01i6y

profile_bio.description: Rare Earths, Critical Minerals & Geopolitics
